dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a chest pain call at a clinic.
Hello, I'm Taylor with the rescue squad. What prompted you to call 911 today?
The patient is a 73-year-old man with chest pain that started about a week ago and has continued during his visit for a toenail trim.
He describes it as a burning sensation, an 8 out of 10, located specifically in his left chest without radiating anywhere else.
I'm alert and oriented. I know today is the 2nd of September and I'm currently in the clinic.
Can you use your arms and move your legs normally, sir?
Your airway is patent and clear. I observe no signs of obstruction or distress.
We are monitoring your respirations now. You are breathing at a normal rate and depth of 16 breaths per minute.
Your circulation appears normal with strong radial pulses. Your skin is warm, dry, and shows a healthy pink color.
You mentioned the pain worsens when lying flat but stays localized. What other symptoms have you experienced?
The pain was a 10 on Sunday but is now at 8. I've had no fever, chills, shortness of breath, cough, or abdominal discomfort.
Have you recently experienced trauma, had a fall, or had contact with someone with possible or confirmed COVID-19?
No, I haven't undergone any trauma or had contact with anyone with the virus.
You mentioned pain levels of 10 on Sunday to 8 now. Does it have a burning quality, and are there any other associated symptoms?
It remains burning; there is no nausea or diarrhea.
We are placing you on a 4-lead cardiac monitor for baseline readings.
Now we'll apply a 12-lead ECG for further evaluation. Findings include right bundle branch block and signs consistent with right ventricular hypertrophy.
I will now establish an IV in your arm for potential medications or fluids.
Your initial vital signs: blood pressure is 143 over 89, heart rate is 105, and respiratory rate is 16.
Your oxygen saturation is 99%. Have you experienced any shortness of breath?
Your pulse is 105 with sinus tachycardia detected. Blood glucose was measured at 153.
You're alert, oriented to person, place, time, and event, and your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15.
No edema in arms or legs. Your resting posture is upright with semi-Fowler's support.
Have you noticed any abdominal discomfort or tenderness during this time?
No, I haven't had any stomach issues.
Have you had any back pain or abdominal pain alongside the chest pain?
No, just the chest pain.
I'll check your motor skills and sensation now. I need you to squeeze my fingers and move your toes on command, okay?
Understood.
Everything looks good. You have full motor function and sensation in both arms and legs.
We note you have a history of chronic conditions, including COPD, hyperlipidemia, chronic pancreatitis, PVD, and primary hypertension.
We note you have an allergy to aspirin.
You also have a history of alcohol abuse in remission and diastolic heart failure.
Your skin is warm, dry, and pink, with normal capillary refill. There are no rashes or petechiae present.
Both pupils are 3 mm, equal, and reactive to light. There's no discharge or signs of facial pressure.
Your chest rises evenly with normal bilateral breath sounds. The EKG reveals a right bundle branch block with widened complexes.
There's normal chest wall movement and no visible abnormal chest wall deformities.
Reassessment of blood glucose shows a 0. How are your energy levels today?
Your vital signs are now 131 over 86, heart rate 99, and oxygen saturation 94%.
You show normal posture and use of extremities. No deformity or swelling noted.
Are you experiencing any difficulty with breathing at rest?
The EKG also shows right ventricular hypertrophy and late transition signs in some leads.
We will transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] with advance notice to the hospital staff.
You are secure in a semi-Fowler's position on the stretcher for the transfer.
I will recheck your vital signs during transport to monitor for any changes.
Your repeat blood pressure is 124 over 82, heart rate 97, and respiratory rate remains at 16.
Oxygen saturation is now at 97%. Did the pain change at all while we were monitoring?
It's stayed at 8 out of 10, no related symptoms have changed.
I repeated the motor testing, and again you are full PMS in all four limbs.
Hospital has been notified with your condition and arrival expected shortly.
You're comfortable and supported for transport now. Let me know if you need something.
Your final vitals before arriving are a blood pressure of 131 over 84, heart rate 99.
Oxygen saturation is once again at 96%. No abnormal vital signs noted during transport.
Let me confirm-no nausea, back pain, vomiting, or abdominal discomfort at all?
No changes in reported symptoms. Still no new complaints.
Hospital staff are waiting for your transfer. I'll finish securing the ambulance while we move.
Ready, you're moving to the hospital now with continuous monitoring in place.
IV site is secure, no infiltration. I will maintain IV access for potential medications.
Pain remains 8/10 with burning nature, no urticaria or changes in skin condition.
Your condition remains stable with no new complaints since your last assessment.
Rechecked and confirmed-your airway remains open and without distress.
Skin condition hasn't changed, remains warm, dry, and pink with no new lesions or rashes.
Pupils remain equal, round, and reactive to light, consistency good.
Chest movement is equal, breathing unlabored with regular breath sounds and normal effort.
Hospital ED is prepared to take care of you. Let me check your wrist IV for compliance.
Everything checks out. You're safely moving to the hospital for further evaluation.
Repeat vitals are a pulse of 96, and an oxygen level of 93%. No concerning changes.
You're set now, being moved safely. Let me know if you need anything else.
We've arrived. Hospital is waiting for your complete handover now.
You are fully secured for hospital transfer. Transport care will now be handed over to staff.
Breathing is unlabored with equal depth and rate. No stridor or wheeze noted.
No tenderness or discoloration noted in the abdominal area.
I repeated the full abdominal assessment-hollow contour, non-tender, soft, and non-distended.
Hospital received a complete pre-arrival report with your history, ECG, and current status.
Patient to hospital transfer complete. Your care will be rotated to the emergency department now.
Vitals were steady during the trip. Final vitals before hospital entry are BP 131/86.
Nitro and other medications appropriately used before our arrival with relief noted to 0/10 prior.
The bed height lowers to meet the hospital stretcher. Please stay still during transfer.
Transferring full care now. Please remain in semi-Fowler for arrival.
We'll leave the IV-running line in place for hospital to resume care.
Patient steady upon arrival. Time-stamped records sent - event closed.
Contact made with hospital, bed level equalized, ready for safe transfer.
All responsibilities transfers now. Thank you for your cooperation today.
Final check shows full response to treatment, vitals in controlled range.
Patient shows full A&O x4 (person, place, time, and event) now and responding appropriately.
Confirmed: No recent N/V/D, back, abdominal or chest changes apart from initial given.
Patient arrives at the ER in stable condition, with complete care handed over.
Hospital has full patient record and IV line. Handoff complete with this note.
Final BP before hand over reads 128/80. No relevant changes in protocol needed.
No life-threatening conditions noted in care time. Managing based on clinic triage.
Repeat oxygen at 95%. No signs of hypoxia or deterioration.
No additional allergens reported from medical profile.
All vital signs consistent over transport, no additional drug administered per life threat.
All IV rules followed. 20g in good site. Continue O2 if needed during ER.
Patient now at triage. Contact made. No issues with movement into staging.
All transport protocols followed. Transfer file complete and copy to agency.
Vehicle ready for return or next assignment. Patient safely handled.
All emergency protocols followed during transport. Vital signs stable as per documentation.
Patient exited without incident. Medically stable and moved into emergency care.
Final check done with full patient data for unlined shift ending. Ready for repeat.
All steps reviewed in protocol 2020-2434 (chamber time). No red flags saved.
No after events noted post-transport. Protocol files are complete in submission.
Moved 73 year old male to care with chest pain. Full documentation on submission to hospital.
No complications in care; start of care review final. Transfer best practices followed.
